Kneeling removes all lower-body cheating, so you really have to own each rep — don't let the weight pull you forward out of position.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15/side, unilateral lat emphasis. Noa: Kneeling eliminates lower back involvement. Boris: 3x12/side, pair with bilateral pulldown.

exercises.name: Seated Barbell Overhead Press
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on a bench with a low back or a rack set to the correct height. Unrack the bar at collarbone height and press it straight up overhead until your arms lock out. Lower it back to your collar bones in a controlled manner. Don't let the bar drift forward — keep it over your base of support and push your head through at the top.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, strict shoulder overload. Noa: Lower lumbar load vs standing OHP. Boris: 3x10, excellent for shoulder hypertrophy block.

exercises.name: Z-Press
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor with your legs extended straight out in front of you, holding a barbell (or dumbbells) at shoulder height. Press the weight directly overhead and lower it back down. Because you have no back support or leg drive, this demands serious core stability and shoulder mobility. Don't round your lower back to compensate — if you can't stay upright, reduce the weight.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-10 reps, pure overhead pressing strength. Noa: High mobility demand — needs hamstring flexibility. Boris: 3x8, excellent OHP movement quality check.

exercises.name: Cable Front Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a single handle or straight bar to a low pulley. Stand facing away from the machine and hold the handle(s) in front of your thighs. Raise the cable up to shoulder height, keeping your arms nearly straight, then lower slowly. Don't lean back or use your hips — the movement is all shoulder.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, anterior delt isolation. Noa: Low joint stress. Boris: 3x15, only add if pressing is insufficient for front delt.

exercises.name: Machine Lateral Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in the machine and place the back of your upper arms or elbows on the pads. Raise your arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height, then lower slowly. The machine eliminates wrist and grip fatigue, letting you focus purely on the side delts. Don't shrug your shoulders during the movement.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps, best for high-volume lateral delt work. Noa: Very safe, fixed path. Boris: 3-4x15-20, excellent for shoulder width.

exercises.name: Cable Upright Row
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a straight bar or rope to a low pulley. Stand close to the machine and grip the bar with an overhand grip slightly narrower than shoulder-width. Pull the bar up toward your chin, elbows flaring out to the sides, then lower slowly. Don't pull the bar all the way to your nose — stopping at chin height is enough and keeps the shoulders safe.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, lateral delt + upper trap. Noa: Wide grip essential — narrow grip impingement risk. Boris: 3x12, moderate load.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Apart
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a resistance band in front of you at chest height with both hands, arms straight and hands shoulder-width apart. Pull the band apart by moving both hands outward to the sides until the band touches your chest, then return slowly. Keep your arms straight throughout. Don't let your shoulders roll forward — stand tall with your chest up.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps, posterior shoulder health. Noa: Essential prehab for heavy pressers. Boris: 2-3x20 every session before pressing.

exercises.name: Pistol Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on one leg and extend the other leg straight out in front of you. Lower your body down on the standing leg, keeping your extended leg off the floor, until your thigh goes below parallel — or as deep as you can. Drive through your heel to stand back up. Don't let your heel come off the ground or your torso collapse forward; both are signs you need more mobility or strength work before going deeper.
exercises.committeeNotes: Elite unilateral leg strength.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Press
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in the leg press machine and place only one foot in the centre of the platform, foot about hip-width from the edge. Lower the weight until your knee reaches about 90 degrees, then press back up without fully locking the knee out. Keep your lower back pressed into the seat throughout. Don't let your lower back peel away from the pad as you go deep — shorten your range before you let that happen.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15/side, unilateral quad focus. Noa: Safe, no axial loading. Boris: 3x12/side, excellent post-injury rehabilitation.

exercises.name: Belt Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Clip the belt around your hips and stand on the elevated platforms so the weight hangs below you. With hands free or lightly touching the frame for balance, squat straight down keeping your torso upright, then drive back up through your heels. Because the load is on your hips rather than your spine, you can go deep and focus entirely on your legs. Don't lean on the support frame for help — use it only for balance, not to take load off your legs.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, quad-dominant without spinal load. Noa: Best alternative when back cannot tolerate barbell. Boris: High-volume blocks, 4x15.

exercises.name: Leg Press Calf Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in a leg press machine with the balls of your feet on the very bottom edge of the platform, legs nearly straight. Push through the balls of your feet to extend your ankles as far as possible, hold at the top, then lower your heels for a full stretch. Keep the range full on every rep — both the stretch at the bottom and the peak contraction at the top matter. Don't rush through the reps; calves respond best to slow, deliberate movement through the full range.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps, seated position hits soleus more. Noa: Very safe position. Boris: 4x20, high volume for calf hypertrophy.

exercises.name: Cable Pull-Through
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a rope handle to the low pulley, face away from the cable stack and grab the rope between your legs. Hinge at your hips and push them back, letting the rope go back between your legs, then drive your hips through hard to stand up tall. Squeeze your glutes at the top of each rep. Don't use your arms to pull the weight — your arms are just holding the rope; all the power should come from your hips snapping forward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, hip hinge teaching tool + glute/hamstring. Noa: Very safe, excellent for beginners learning deadlift pattern. Boris: 3x15, warm-up for deadlift day.

exercises.name: Landmine Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the end of a landmine bar at chest height with both hands, feet shoulder-width apart and toes out slightly. The angled bar helps you stay upright naturally as you squat down and back up. Drive through your heels and keep your elbows up to maintain the bar position. Don't let the bar drift away from your chest — keeping it close is what makes this variation so good for an upright torso.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, beginner-friendly compound squat. Noa: Low spinal load. Boris: 3x12, higher reps work well.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Clean
exercises.setupNotes: Start with one kettlebell on the floor between your feet, hinge down and grip the handle. Drive through your legs and hips explosively to pull the bell upward, then guide it smoothly into the rack position on your forearm and shoulder — the bell should land softly, not bang your wrist. Lower it back to the floor or swing it back for the next rep. Don't let the bell flip over and crash onto your wrist — the trick is to keep it close to your body and guide it into position rather than letting it fly.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-8/side, explosive hip power. Noa: Wrist injury risk if rack catch not cued. Boris: 3x5/side, technique before load.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Press
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a kettlebell in a rack position — resting on the back of your wrist and forearm at shoulder height. Press it straight up overhead until your arm is fully extended and your bicep is next to your ear. Lower it back to the rack position. Don't let your wrist fold back — keep it straight so the bell rests on your forearm, not dangling from your hand.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-8/side, excellent shoulder stability. Noa: High rotator cuff demand — beneficial for shoulder health. Boris: 3x6/side, technique focus.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Snatch
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the kettlebell in front of you with one hand and hike it back between your legs like a swing. Drive your hips forward to swing it up, then as it reaches shoulder height, punch your hand through and under it to catch it in a locked-out overhead position. Lower it back down by letting it swing back between your legs. Don't muscle the bell up with your arm — the power comes from your hips and the hip-hinge pattern.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-10/side, total body power and conditioning. Noa: High technique demand, learn with light KB. Boris: 3x5/side, test with 5min/100 rep challenge.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Row
exercises.setupNotes: Place one hand on a bench for support, hold a kettlebell in the other hand and let it hang straight down. Row the kettlebell up toward your hip by driving your elbow back, squeezing your shoulder blade at the top. Lower it to a full stretch and repeat. Don't let the kettlebell's odd weight distribution pull your wrist into a weird angle — keep your wrist straight and neutral throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-12/side, good lat + rhomboid. Noa: Lower back demand moderate — brace core. Boris: 3x10/side, great for home gym or travel.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.setupNotes: Hold one or two kettlebells in front of your thighs, standing tall with a slight bend in your knees. Push your hips back and lower the kettlebells down your legs, back flat and chest up, until you feel a big stretch in your hamstrings. Drive your hips forward to return to standing and squeeze your glutes at the top. Don't let the kettlebells swing away from your legs — keeping them close maximises hamstring tension throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, hamstring and glute hinge. Noa: Low spinal load vs barbell. Boris: 3x12, good for beginners learning hip hinge.

exercises.name: Band Bicep Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on a resistance band with both feet and hold one end in each hand with an underhand grip. Curl both hands up to shoulder height, keeping your elbows at your sides, then lower slowly. The band provides increasing resistance as you approach the top. Don't let the band pull your elbows forward — keep them pinned.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-20 reps, home gym bicep option. Noa: Very safe, low joint stress. Boris: 3x15-20, excellent for travel or deload weeks.

exercises.name: Band Tricep Pushdown
exercises.setupNotes: Loop a resistance band around a fixed anchor above you. Grip the bottom end with both hands using an overhand grip. Keeping your elbows at your sides, push your hands down until your arms are straight, then return slowly. Don't lean into the band — keep your torso upright and let only your forearms move.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 20-30 reps, excellent pump. Noa: Very safe for elbows. Boris: 3x25, finisher or home workout.

exercises.name: Band Hip Thrust
exercises.setupNotes: Sit with your upper back against a bench, loop a resistance band across your hips and anchor each end under your hands or the bench feet. Drive through your heels to thrust your hips up, squeezing your glutes hard at the top. Lower back down slowly and repeat. Don't let the band pull your hips to one side — keep the tension centred and your hips level throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps, peak glute contraction stimulus. Noa: Very safe. Boris: 3x20, warm-up or accessory for heavier hip thrusts.

exercises.name: Band Lateral Walk
exercises.setupNotes: Loop a resistance band around your legs just above your knees or around your ankles, stand with knees slightly bent and feet hip-width apart. Step sideways with one foot and then bring the other foot in, maintaining constant tension on the band throughout. Keep your chest up, back straight, and hips low the whole time. Don't let your feet come together completely on each step — keep some width so the band stays taut and your glutes stay working.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-20 steps/side, glute medius activation. Noa: Essential knee health prehab for squatters. Boris: 2-3x15/side before lower body session.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Apart (Resistance Band)
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a resistance band in front of you at about shoulder height with arms straight and hands about shoulder-width apart. Pull the band apart by squeezing your shoulder blades together until the band touches your chest. Slowly return to the start and repeat. Don't let your elbows bend as you pull — keep them nearly straight; bending them turns it into a back-fly and takes tension off the target muscles.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 20-25 reps, posterior chain shoulder health. Noa: Critical for heavy pressers — prevents AC joint issues. Boris: 3x20 every upper body session.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Set the bar across your upper back in the Smith machine, feet slightly in front of the bar at shoulder width with toes turned out slightly. Unrack the bar and squat down until your thighs are at least parallel, keeping your chest up. Drive back up through your heels and re-rack when done. Don't place your feet directly under the bar like a free-bar squat — the fixed bar path means you need your feet a bit further forward to keep a natural squat pattern.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, controlled overload. Noa: Fixed path changes natural biomechanics — not a free squat replacement. Boris: Use for volume work, not primary strength.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Overhead Press
exercises.setupNotes: Set the bar to shoulder height and sit (or stand) underneath it. Unrack and press straight up, then lower back to shoulder height. The fixed bar path means you don't need to stabilize laterally. Don't jam your chin forward as the bar passes your face — tuck your chin back so the bar can travel straight up.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, controlled shoulder overload. Noa: Fixed path reduces rotator cuff demand — consider as secondary press. Boris: 3x10, drop sets effective.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Romanian Deadlift
exercises.setupNotes: Set the bar at hip height in the Smith machine, grip it at shoulder width and stand tall in front of it. Push your hips back and lower the bar down your legs, keeping your back flat and the bar skimming close to your thighs. Drive your hips forward to stand back up. Don't let the fixed bar path throw off your alignment — if it feels awkward, adjust your foot position slightly until the movement feels natural.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, hamstring isolation. Noa: Vertical bar path differs from free RDL. Boris: 3x12, moderate load.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Set the bar across your upper back, stand a couple of feet in front of the machine, and place one foot back on a bench. Lower your back knee straight toward the floor, keeping your front torso upright, then drive back up through your front heel. The Smith machine's fixed path helps with balance, so you can really focus on working the front leg. Don't let the bar's fixed path force your torso into a weird angle — adjust your foot position until the descent feels natural.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12/side, heavy unilateral loading. Noa: Easier to balance than free-weight version. Boris: 3x10/side, excellent for strength phase.

exercises.name: Power Clean
exercises.setupNotes: Start with the bar on the floor, feet hip-width apart, and grip just outside your legs. Pull the bar explosively by driving through your legs, then shrug your shoulders and pull your elbows up and around to catch the bar on your front shoulders in a partial squat. Stand up to complete the rep, then lower the bar back down with control. Don't rely on your arms to muscle the bar up — the power comes entirely from the explosive leg drive and hip snap; your arms just guide the bar into position.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: High threshold motor unit recruitment. Noa: Requires excellent wrist and shoulder rack mobility. Boris: Use for explosive power, low reps (1-5).

exercises.name: Barbell Snatch
exercises.setupNotes: Use a wide grip on the bar, pull it explosively from the floor with a big leg drive, then receive it overhead with locked arms in a squat position in one fluid movement. Stand up with the bar locked out overhead to complete the lift. This is a technically demanding Olympic lift — get coaching before loading it heavy. Don't muscle the bar up with your arms; the bar has to be moving fast enough from leg drive alone to get it overhead — your arms just lock it out at the top.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Total body coordination and power. Noa: High technical demand; shoulder stability is paramount. Boris: Focus on speed and technique over load.

exercises.name: Clean and Jerk
exercises.setupNotes: Pull the bar from the floor explosively and catch it on your front shoulders in a squat (the clean), stand up, then dip slightly and drive the bar overhead catching it with locked arms (the jerk). This is the king of Olympic lifts and requires proper coaching to learn safely. Focus on leg drive for both the clean pull and the jerk — your legs do the heavy lifting, your arms just complete it. Don't try to muscle the weight up — if you can't get under the bar fast enough, the weight is too heavy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Ultimate metabolic and power demand. Noa: Heavy impact on joints; ensure proper footwear. Boris: The king of Oly lifts for total strength.

exercises.name: Muscle-Up (Rings)
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from rings at full extension and perform an explosive pull-up, then transition your hands from pulling below the rings to pressing above them in one fluid movement. Finish in a ring dip position with arms locked out. This is an advanced movement — you need solid pull-ups and ring dips before attempting it. Don't use a massive kip to throw yourself up — work on building the pull and transition strength separately before combining them.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Incredible upper body pulling and pushing integration. Noa: High stress on the medial elbow and rotator cuff. Boris: Advanced skill move.

exercises.name: Handstand Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Kick up into a handstand against a wall, hands shoulder-width apart. Lower yourself by bending your elbows until the top of your head lightly touches the floor, then press back up to full lockout. Keep your core tight and your body as vertical as possible. Don't let your elbows flare out too wide — a slight inward angle is safer for the shoulders.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Bodyweight equivalent to a heavy OHP. Noa: Maintain neutral neck; avoid 'craning' to look at floor. Boris: Progress to deficits for more ROM.

exercises.name: Burpee (Standard)
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, then drop your hands to the floor and jump or step your feet back into a push-up position. Do a push-up, then jump your feet back toward your hands, stand up, and jump with your arms overhead at the top. Move through each position deliberately — it should be a push-up, not a chest flop. Don't skip the push-up or let your hips sag on the way down; every rep should look controlled even when you're gassed.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: High metabolic cost. Noa: Watch lower back 'sag' during the push-up phase. Boris: Use in AMRAP or EMOM formats for conditioning.

exercises.name: Toes-to-Bar
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar with a firm overhand grip and your shoulders packed — no dead-arm dangling. From a controlled hang, engage your stomach and swing your legs up, bringing your toes all the way up to touch the bar. Lower them back down with control before the next rep. Don't use a wild kip to fling your feet up — if you can't touch the bar with control, work on hanging knee raises first.
exercises.committeeNotes: Complete core and grip.

exercises.name: Wall Ball Shot
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a medicine ball at chest height, stand a foot or two from the wall, feet shoulder-width apart. Squat down and then explode upward, using your whole body to throw the ball up to a target on the wall. Catch it as it comes back down and flow straight into the next squat. Don't just use your arms to throw — the power should come from your legs driving up; your arms just guide it.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Full body power and endurance. Noa: Keep chest up to avoid ball pulling you forward. Boris: High rep conditioning (20-50 reps).

exercises.name: Kettlebell Thruster
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a kettlebell in the rack position at shoulder height. Squat down to full depth, then as you stand, use the momentum of the squat to drive the bell overhead in one smooth movement. Lower it back to the rack as you descend into the next squat. Don't press from the top of the squat independently — the squat and press should feel like one continuous movement.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Massive metabolic demand and total body tension. Noa: Don't let KBs pull shoulders into internal rotation. Boris: Use for high-intensity circuits.

exercises.name: Overhead Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the bar locked out straight overhead with a wide grip, arms fully extended, feet shoulder-width apart and toes out. Keeping the bar directly over your feet the whole way, squat as deep as your mobility allows, then drive back up. This demands serious shoulder mobility and ankle flexibility — start very light. Don't let the bar drift forward out of line with your feet; if it drifts, your shoulders or ankles need more mobility work first.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: The ultimate test of mobility and stability. Noa: Requires excellent thoracic and ankle mobility. Boris: Use for stability and Oly prep.

exercises.name: Arch-Body Hold (Superman)
exercises.setupNotes: Lie face-down on the floor with arms extended overhead. Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor by squeezing your glutes and back muscles. Hold the position for the target time or reps, breathing steadily. Don't strain your neck by craning it up — keep your head in line with your spine and look straight down at the floor.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Targets the entire posterior chain isometrically. Noa: Avoid neck hyperextension; look at floor. Boris: Essential for postural health.

exercises.name: Chest-to-Bar Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar with an overhand grip and pull yourself up with enough force that your chest actually touches the bar at the top. Lower back to a full hang before each rep. This requires significantly more pulling strength than a regular chin-over-bar pull-up — build your pull-ups to 15+ reps before targeting this. Don't kip wildly to get your chest there — a controlled hip drive is fine, but a full kip-and-whip is a different skill entirely.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Increased ROM for greater lat and trap recruitment. Noa: High demand on shoulder internal rotation at the top. Boris: CrossFit staple.

exercises.name: Ring Dip
exercises.setupNotes: Grip the rings and support yourself above them. Lower yourself by bending your elbows while leaning your torso slightly forward, keeping the rings close to your body. Press back up to the starting position, turning the rings out at the top for stability. Don't allow the rings to drift out wide — keep them tight to your sides on the way down.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Instability massively increases pec and tricep recruitment. Noa: High risk for shoulder strain if rings drift. Boris: Elite strength movement.

exercises.name: Front-Rack Reverse Lunge
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the bar across your front shoulders with elbows up high (front rack position), then step one foot back and lower your back knee toward the floor. Keep your torso upright and your front shin vertical, then drive through your front heel to return to standing. The front-loaded position makes staying upright harder — that's the point. Don't let your elbows drop when you lunge — once the elbows fall, the bar rolls and your torso folds forward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: High quad and core demand vs back-loaded. Noa: Safer for those with lower back issues. Boris: 8-10 reps per leg.

exercises.name: Box Step-Over
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and stand beside a box. Step one foot up onto the box, drive through that heel to bring your body over the top, then step down on the other side. Turn around and repeat in the opposite direction for each rep. Don't rush — control each step up and over, and make sure the working leg does the driving rather than you hoisting yourself with momentum.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Great for glute and quad endurance. Noa: Control the descent to save the Achilles. Boris: Common CrossFit conditioning piece.

exercises.name: American Kettlebell Swing
exercises.setupNotes: Perform a standard kettlebell swing but instead of stopping at chest height, continue swinging the bell all the way overhead until your arms are fully extended. The same explosive hip drive powers the movement — your arms stay straight as the bell arcs overhead. Lower it back through the same arc and hinge into the next rep. Don't muscle the bell overhead with your arms — if you can't get it there with hip power alone, use a lighter weight.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: More shoulder ROM than Russian version. Noa: Avoid over-arching the lower back at the top. Boris: High power output required.

exercises.name: Bear Crawl
exercises.setupNotes: Start on all fours with your hands under your shoulders, knees under your hips, and knees hovering about an inch off the floor. Move forward by stepping your right hand and left foot simultaneously, then your left hand and right foot — keeping your back flat and hips low the whole time. Don't let your hips rise up into the air; the height of your hips should barely change as you crawl.
exercises.committeeNotes: Full body coordination and core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Devil Press
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with two dumbbells on the floor in front of you. Hinge down, grab them, jump your feet back into a push-up position, and do a push-up. Jump your feet back toward your hands, then use your hip drive to swing both dumbbells from the floor straight up overhead in one fluid motion — like a swing-to-press. Don't press the dumbbells up slowly from your shoulders; the momentum from your hip drive is what makes this movement efficient.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: Absolute metabolic killer. Noa: Spine must stay neutral during the high-velocity pull. Boris: Low rep conditioning (5-10).

exercises.name: Tuck Sit
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor (or parallel bars/handles) and plant your hands beside your hips. Press through your palms to lift your seat off the surface, then draw your knees up toward your chest and hold. Your hips should be higher than your knees, and your arms should be straight. Don't let your shoulders creep up toward your ears — keep them packed down as you press.
exercises.committeeNotes: Regressed version of L-sit.

exercises.name: Handstand Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Kick up against a wall and find your balance with arms fully locked and your body as straight as possible — not banana-shaped. Hold the position, breathing steadily. Over time, work to reduce wall contact and aim for a free-standing balance. Don't stare at the floor right below you — look at a spot slightly in front of your hands for better balance.
exercises.committeeNotes: Shoulder stability and balance.

exercises.name: Pseudo-Planche Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Get into a push-up position with your hands at hip level, fingers pointing back or down. Lean forward so your shoulders are well in front of your hands. Lock your arms straight and hold, keeping your body rigid and as horizontal as possible. Don't let your hips sag — strong core tension is essential to maintain the body line.
exercises.committeeNotes: Builds straight-arm shoulder strength.

exercises.name: Horse Stance
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet very wide, toes pointed out, and lower yourself into a deep squat until your thighs are parallel or below. Hold the position with your chest up, back straight, and hands on your thighs or pressed together in front of you. Breathe steadily and sink a little deeper as your hips open up over time. Don't let your knees collapse inward — actively push them out in line with your toes throughout the hold.
exercises.committeeNotes: Traditional martial arts leg conditioning.

exercises.name: Duck Walk
exercises.setupNotes: Get into a deep squat with feet shoulder-width apart, toes out, thighs below parallel. Walk forward while staying in the squat position, taking small steps and keeping your torso upright. Stay low the entire time — no bobbing up and down with each step. Don't let your heels come up off the floor; if your heels lift, your squat depth or ankle mobility needs work.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hip mobility and leg endurance.

exercises.name: Bird-Dog
exercises.setupNotes: Start on all fours with your hands under your shoulders and knees under your hips. Brace your stomach, then simultaneously extend your right arm straight forward and your left leg straight back until both are parallel to the floor. Hold for a moment, return to the start, and repeat on the other side. Don't let your lower back rotate or your hips tilt — your pelvis should stay perfectly level throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Core stability and spinal health.

exercises.name: Pallof Press (Iso-Band)
exercises.setupNotes: Anchor a resistance band at chest height to a rack or post, and stand side-on to the anchor point with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the band at your chest with both hands, set your stance, and press your arms straight out in front of you — hold the position for the prescribed time before returning. The band will constantly try to pull you sideways; your job is to resist it. Don't rotate your torso or shift your weight toward the anchor — stay square and still.
exercises.committeeNotes: Anti-rotation stability.

exercises.name: Archer Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a wide push-up stance. As you lower yourself, shift your weight to one side while the other arm stays extended straight — that extended arm assists balance while the bent arm does most of the work. Alternate sides each rep. Don't let your hips rotate — keep your body square to the floor throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Unilateral push strength.

exercises.name: Typewriter Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Begin in a wide push-up stance and lower yourself to the bottom. At the bottom, slide your chest horizontally from one hand to the other before pressing back up — like a typewriter carriage moving across. Keep your hips still and your body low throughout the slide. Don't rush the transition; slow, controlled movement makes it effective.
exercises.committeeNotes: Time under tension and control.

exercises.name: Pseudo-Planche Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Get into a push-up position with your hands at your hips instead of your shoulders, fingers pointing down or backward. Lean forward so your shoulders are ahead of your hands. Perform a push-up from this position, keeping your body rigid. Don't let your hips sag — this requires strong core engagement to maintain the forward-lean position.
exercises.committeeNotes: Planche progression.

exercises.name: Clap Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a standard push-up position. Lower yourself to the bottom, then explode up as powerfully as you can, pushing hard enough that your hands leave the floor. Clap your hands together in the air and land softly back in the push-up position. Don't try to clap too high — focus on speed and a quick, soft landing to protect your wrists.
exercises.committeeNotes: Plyometric pushing power.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Set your feet wide and place one hand in the center of your chest on the floor, other arm behind your back. Lower your chest toward your hand while keeping your hips as square to the floor as possible, then push back up. Expect some hip rotation — it's natural, but minimize it. Don't let your elbow flare out wide; keep it tucked close to your body.
exercises.committeeNotes: Elite unilateral strength.

exercises.name: Staggered-Hand Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Set up in a push-up position with one hand at the normal shoulder position and the other hand several inches forward. Lower into the push-up and press back up. Switch hand positions each set to keep both sides balanced. Don't let the forward hand pull your shoulder forward — keep both shoulders level throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Varied pec and shoulder loading.

exercises.name: Tiger Bend Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a forearm plank position. Push up from your forearms to your hands one arm at a time, then lower back to your forearms. The movement transitions: forearms → hands → back to forearms repeatedly. Keep your body rigid and hips level throughout. Don't let your hips sag or rotate — this should feel like a moving plank.
exercises.committeeNotes: Tricep and shoulder focus.

exercises.name: Bench Dip (Feet Elevated)
exercises.setupNotes: Place your hands on a bench behind you and your heels on another bench or box in front of you so your body is suspended between them. Lower yourself by bending your elbows until your upper arms are parallel to the floor, then push back up. The elevated feet increase the load compared to standard bench dips. Don't let your hips drift far away from the bench — keep your back close and dip straight down.
exercises.committeeNotes: Increased tricep load.

exercises.name: Incline Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Place your hands on an elevated surface — a bench, a box, or even a wall — and get into a push-up position. Lower your chest toward the surface, then push back up. The higher the surface, the easier the movement, making this a great starting point. Don't let your hips drop or sag — keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
exercises.committeeNotes: Easier variation, lower chest focus.

exercises.name: Hindu Push-up
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a downward dog position. Lower your chest toward the floor in a sweeping dive, then scoop through into an upward dog or cobra position with your hips low. Reverse the motion to get back to the start. The movement is a flowing wave — forward on the way in, backward on the way out. Don't stop at the bottom — this is a fluid, continuous movement.
exercises.committeeNotes: Shoulder and spinal mobility.

exercises.name: DB Around the World
exercises.setupNotes: Lie flat on a bench holding two light dumbbells at your thighs. Keeping a slight bend in your elbows, sweep both dumbbells out to the sides and up overhead in a wide arc, then bring them together above your face, then reverse the arc back down. Think of it as a big circle around your body. Don't go heavy — this is a mobility and control exercise, not a strength movement.
exercises.committeeNotes: Complete shoulder mobility and health.

exercises.name: Around the World (Pull-up Bar)
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar with a shoulder-width grip. Perform a circular motion by pulling up on one side, rotating your body at the top, and lowering on the other side — then reverse the circle. Keep the movement controlled and your body as stable as possible. Don't swing your legs or use momentum to get around; the harder you control the arc, the more you get out of it.
exercises.committeeNotes: Elite core and oblique strength.

exercises.name: Negative Pull-up
exercises.setupNotes: Use a box or jump to get your chin above the bar in the top pull-up position, then lower yourself as slowly as possible — aiming for 3–5 seconds per rep. Step back up and repeat without trying to pull yourself back up. This is one of the best ways to build the strength needed for your first full pull-up. Don't drop fast — the whole benefit comes from the slow, controlled lowering phase; a fast drop is a wasted rep.
exercises.committeeNotes: Building pull-up strength.

exercises.name: Commando Pull-up
exercises.setupNotes: Grip the bar with one hand in front of the other (like you're holding a baseball bat), hang with arms extended. Pull yourself up so your head goes to one side of the bar, lower down, then pull up so your head goes to the other side on the next rep. Alternate sides each rep. Don't let your body spin and lose tension — keep your core tight and control the rotation.
exercises.committeeNotes: Bicep and brachialis focus.

exercises.name: Archer Pull-up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar with a wide overhand grip and pull yourself toward one hand, keeping that arm bent and the other arm as straight as possible. Lower back to the center and pull toward the other side on the next rep. This is a step toward the one-arm pull-up and is much harder than regular pull-ups — build serious pull-up volume first. Don't swing to compensate for the difficulty — if you can't control it, narrow your grip width until you can.
exercises.committeeNotes: Unilateral pull strength.

exercises.name: Australian Pull-up (Inverted Row)
exercises.setupNotes: Set a bar in a rack at about waist height, lie underneath it and grip it with both hands at shoulder width. Keep your body in a straight line from heels to head and pull your chest up to the bar by driving your elbows back. Lower back to straight arms and repeat. Don't let your hips sag — your core keeps everything in a plank; if your hips drop, shorten the range by bending your knees.
exercises.committeeNotes: Foundational back strength.

exercises.name: Face Pull (Resistance Band)
exercises.setupNotes: Anchor a resistance band at about head height and grab both ends with an overhand grip. Pull the band toward your face by driving your elbows back and out wide, separating your hands at the end of the movement. Pause briefly when your hands are beside your ears, then return with control. Don't pull the band to your chin or neck — aim for your forehead or eyes height, with elbows flared out and high.
exercises.committeeNotes: Rear delt and rotator cuff.

exercises.name: L-Sit Pull-up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar and raise your legs to parallel with the floor (L-position), keeping them there throughout the set. Pull yourself up with your chin clearing the bar while maintaining the L position, then lower back to a full hang. The core demand of holding your legs up makes every rep significantly harder. Don't let your legs drop as soon as the pulling gets hard — if you can't hold both at once, build your L-sit and pull-ups separately first.
exercises.committeeNotes: Combined pull and core strength.

exercises.name: Explosive Pull-up (Chest-to-Bar)
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar and perform a pull-up with maximum power, pulling hard enough that your chest makes contact with the bar at the top. Lower back down to a full hang and rest as needed between reps to maintain quality. Focus on accelerating through the entire pull, not just the bottom. Don't coast through the top half — the explosive intent needs to be there from the first inch of the pull to get your chest high enough.
exercises.committeeNotes: Power development.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Australian Row
exercises.setupNotes: Set a bar at about waist height, position yourself underneath it and grip with just one hand. Keep your body rigid in a plank and pull your chest up to the bar one-handed, driving your elbow back and squeezing your shoulder blade. Lower back to a straight arm. Keep your body from rotating — brace your core hard to resist the twist that the one-arm loading wants to create.
exercises.committeeNotes: Unilateral pull development.

exercises.name: Scapular Pull-up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar with arms fully extended and pull your shoulder blades down and together without bending your elbows. You'll rise just a couple of inches — this is not a full pull-up; it isolates the muscles that stabilise the start of every pull. Hold briefly at the top, then let your shoulder blades rise back up. Don't bend your elbows at all — if your elbows bend, you're doing a pull-up, not a scapular pull-up.
exercises.committeeNotes: Shoulder health and pull-up prep.

exercises.name: Shrimp Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on one leg and bend the other knee behind you, holding the foot with the same-side hand if needed for balance. Lower your back knee down toward the floor in a controlled single-leg squat, keeping your torso upright. Press through your front heel to stand back up. Don't let your front knee cave inward — drive it out over your toes and keep it there through the whole movement.
exercises.committeeNotes: Unilateral quad and balance.

exercises.name: Skater Lunge
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on one leg and reach your other foot diagonally back behind you and to the side, lowering into a single-leg squat while swinging the opposite arm across your body like a speed skater. Push back up and repeat on the other side, alternating in a skating motion. Keep your chest up and your standing knee tracking over your toes. Don't let your torso collapse forward — keeping your chest up keeps the work in your glutes and quads where it belongs.
exercises.committeeNotes: Lateral power and stability.

exercises.name: Curtsy Lunge
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet hip-width apart, then step one foot diagonally behind and across your body, lowering your back knee toward the floor like a curtsy. Keep your weight on the front foot and your torso upright, then push back to standing. Alternate legs for each rep. Don't let your front knee cave in — it will want to, especially as you fatigue, so actively push it outward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Glute medius focus.

exercises.name: Sissy Squat (Bodyweight)
exercises.setupNotes: Stand holding something lightly for balance, feet together or hip-width apart, and rise onto the balls of your feet. Lean your entire body backward as one unit while bending your knees, lowering until your knees nearly touch the floor. Squeeze your quads hard to pull back up. Don't let your hips break — your body from knee to shoulder should stay in a rigid straight line; any hip bending takes the work away from your quads.
exercises.committeeNotes: Extreme quad isolation.

exercises.name: Reverse Nordic
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel upright with your feet anchored under something sturdy and your body in a straight line from knees to head. Slowly lean your whole body backward from the knees, as far as you can control, keeping your hips extended. Use your quads to resist the fall and pull yourself back upright. Don't let your hips bend as you lean back — the whole body must stay rigid; hip breaking is cheating yourself and removes the quad stretch.
exercises.committeeNotes: Quad stretch and strength.

exercises.name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel on a padded surface and anchor your feet firmly under a bar, machine, or have a partner hold them. Lower your body toward the floor as slowly as possible, fighting gravity with your hamstrings the whole way. Catch yourself at the bottom and use a push-up to assist the return. Don't drop fast — the whole point is the slow, resisted lowering; going slow is what builds the hamstring strength.
exercises.committeeNotes: Elite hamstring strength.

exercises.name: Cossack Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet very wide apart, then shift your weight to one side and squat down on that leg while keeping the other leg straight and foot flat or heel down. Go as deep as your mobility allows, then shift across to the other side. Keep your chest up and your grounded heel flat on the floor. Don't let your grounded heel lift — if it does, your ankle mobility needs work; use a slight heel raise under it until flexibility improves.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hip mobility and strength.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat, then extend one leg straight. Drive through the heel of the planted foot to lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knee. Squeeze your glute hard at the top, then lower back down. Don't let your hips tilt to the non-working side — keep them level throughout by squeezing both glutes, even the one in the air.
exercises.committeeNotes: Unilateral glute focus.

exercises.name: Hip Thrust (Couch/Bench)
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor with your upper back resting against a bench or couch, knees bent and feet flat about hip-width apart. Drive through your heels to lift your hips until your body is in a straight line from shoulders to knees, squeezing your glutes at the top. Lower back down slowly and repeat. Don't hyperextend your lower back at the top — your hips rise until your torso is flat and parallel to the floor, not arching upward beyond that.
exercises.committeeNotes: Maximum glute activation.

exercises.name: Calf Raise (Single-Leg)
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on one foot on the edge of a step with the ball of your foot on the edge and your heel hanging off. Lower your heel as far down as it will go, then rise as high as possible onto the ball of your foot. Hold at the top for a second, then lower slowly. Don't bounce at the bottom or rush the reps — slow and full-range is what actually builds calf size and strength.
exercises.committeeNotes: Unilateral calf strength.

exercises.name: Tibialis Raise (Wall)
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with your back against a wall and heels about a foot out from the base. Lift the fronts of your feet off the floor as high as you can by pulling your toes toward your shins, hold briefly, then lower. Keep the movement controlled and get a full range at both the top and bottom. This targets the shin muscle that often gets neglected — don't rush it; slow deliberate reps are more effective than fast twitchy ones.
exercises.committeeNotes: Anterior shin strength.

exercises.name: Tuck Jump
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, dip into a quarter-squat, then explode upward and pull both knees up toward your chest at the peak of the jump. Land softly on the balls of your feet with bent knees and go straight into the next rep. Stay on the balls of your feet and keep the landings as quiet as possible. Don't land flat-footed or with stiff legs — a loud landing means you're absorbing force with your joints instead of your muscles.
exercises.committeeNotes: Explosive power.

exercises.name: Hollow Rock
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a hollow body hold — lower back flat on the floor, arms overhead, legs extended and lifted, shoulders off the ground. From this rigid position, rock forward and back like a rocking chair, maintaining the exact same body shape throughout. Don't break the position as you rock; if your lower back arches off the floor or your hips bend, reset into hollow before continuing.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dynamic core stability.

exercises.name: V-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Lie flat on your back with your arms extended overhead and legs straight. In one motion, lift your legs and upper body simultaneously, reaching your hands toward your toes to form a V shape at the top. Lower back down with control. Don't use a jerky motion to swing yourself up — the lift should be smooth and driven by your stomach, not momentum.
exercises.committeeNotes: Upper and lower ab focus.

exercises.name: Windshield Wiper (Floor)
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back with your arms extended out to the sides for support, palms down. Lift your legs straight up so they're perpendicular to the floor. Keeping your legs straight and together, slowly lower them to one side until they hover just above the floor, then sweep them back up and over to the other side. Don't let your opposite shoulder lift off the floor — keep both arms pinned to the ground as your legs rotate.
exercises.committeeNotes: Oblique strength.

exercises.name: Windshield Wiper (Bar)
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar with both hands, then pull your legs up so they're at 90 degrees to your torso (like the top of a toes-to-bar). From there, rotate your legs side to side like windshield wipers, controlling the movement through your entire range of motion. Don't drop your legs below parallel or lose tension at the end range — the hardest part is keeping them up and controlled throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Elite oblique strength.

exercises.name: Knee-to-Elbow (Bar)
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip, body in a controlled hang. Bring one knee up and across your body, aiming to touch it to the opposite elbow, then lower it and repeat on the other side. Keep the movement deliberate and rotational — you should feel your obliques working with each rep. Don't just kick your knees up randomly; the cross-body rotation is what makes this exercise effective.
exercises.committeeNotes: Advanced core strength.

exercises.name: Mountain Climber
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a high push-up position with your hands under your shoulders and body in a straight line. Drive one knee toward your chest, then quickly switch legs, keeping your hips down and your core tight throughout. Move at a pace that lets you keep your hips level — not bouncing up and down. Don't let your hips pike up with each knee drive; they should stay at the same height the entire set.
exercises.committeeNotes: Core and conditioning.

exercises.name: Spiderman Plank
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a high push-up position. As you lower into a push-up, bring one knee out to the side toward the same-side elbow, pause briefly, then push back up and return the leg. Alternate sides each rep, keeping your hips level and your core tight through every repetition. Don't let your hips rotate or your lower back arch when you bring the knee out — the movement is in the hip, not the spine.
exercises.committeeNotes: Oblique and core.

exercises.name: Plank Shoulder Tap
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a high push-up position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and your feet also slightly wider for stability. While keeping your hips as still as possible, lift one hand off the floor and tap the opposite shoulder, then return it and repeat on the other side. The goal is to resist rotation — your hips should barely move. Don't let your hips rock side to side with each tap; if they're swinging, widen your stance and slow down.
exercises.committeeNotes: Anti-rotation stability.

exercises.name: Double Under (Jump Rope)
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the jump rope handles at hip height with your elbows close to your sides. Jump with a single, higher-than-normal bounce and flick your wrists quickly so the rope passes under your feet twice before you land. Keep jumps small and wrist-driven — this is about rope speed, not height. Don't jump high and slow thinking it gives you more time; the rope speed comes from your wrists, and excess height just wastes energy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Conditioning and coordination.

exercises.name: Single Under
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the rope handles at hip height, elbows close to your body. Jump with a small, consistent hop — just enough clearance for the rope — and turn the rope with your wrists rather than your whole arms. Keep a steady rhythm and land softly on the balls of your feet. Don't use big arm circles to swing the rope; it should be a light, wrist-driven rotation that stays controlled throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Aerobic conditioning.

exercises.name: Burpee
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, then drop your hands to the floor and jump or step your feet back into a push-up position. Do a push-up, then jump your feet back toward your hands, stand up, and jump with your arms overhead at the top. Move through each position deliberately — it should be a push-up, not a chest flop. Don't skip the push-up or let your hips sag on the way down; every rep should look controlled even when you're gassed.
exercises.committeeNotes: Full body conditioning.

exercises.name: Shadow Boxing
exercises.setupNotes: Stand in a fighting stance with your non-dominant foot forward, hands up at cheek height, and elbows in. Throw punches — jabs, crosses, hooks, uppercuts — in combinations while moving your feet and rotating your hips into each shot. Keep your chin tucked and your non-punching hand up the entire time. Don't drop your guard hand when you throw; every time you punch with one hand, the other should stay protecting your face.
exercises.committeeNotes: Cardio and coordination.

exercises.name: Farmer's Walk (Heavy Bags)
exercises.setupNotes: Pick up two heavy bags or loaded handles — one in each hand — and stand tall with your shoulders pulled back and your core tight. Walk forward with even, controlled steps, keeping your chest up and your posture upright despite the load. Don't let the weight of the bags drag your shoulders forward and down into a hunched position; fight to keep your shoulder blades retracted for the full walk.
exercises.committeeNotes: Grip and posture.

exercises.name: Jumping Jacks
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with your feet together and arms at your sides. Jump your feet out to slightly wider than shoulder-width while simultaneously raising both arms out and up overhead, then jump back to the starting position and repeat at a steady pace. Keep a slight bend in your knees when you land and stay light on your feet. Don't lock your knees straight as you land — absorbing each landing with a soft knee bend reduces impact and keeps you moving smoothly.
exercises.committeeNotes: Warm-up and cardio.
